flashcards.front: What is the formal definition of a graph?
flashcards.back: A graph G = (V, E) where V is a finite non-empty set of vertices and E is a set of edges. Each edge is a pair (u, v) where u and v belong to V.
In any undirected graph, the sum of the degrees of all vertices equals twice the number of edges: Sum(deg(v)) = 2|E|.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a directed and undirected graph?
flashcards.back: In an undirected graph, edge (u,v) = edge (v,u). In a directed graph (digraph), edge <u,v> goes from u to v only; <u,v> and <v,u> are distinct.
A graph where every pair of distinct vertices is connected by an edge. A complete graph on n vertices (Kn) has n(n-1)/2 edges.
Degree of a vertex = number of edges incident on it. In a digraph, in-degree = edges coming in, out-degree = edges going out. For digraphs: sum of in-degrees = sum of out-degrees = |E|.
A graph G' = (V', E') is a subgraph of G = (V, E) if V' is a subset of V and E' is a subset of E, with every edge in E' connecting vertices in V'.
